frameworks.name: Bayesian inference
frameworks.one_line: Bayes' rule combines observations with prior knowledge under limited data

frameworks.name: bounded rationality / resource-rational analysis
frameworks.one_line: Brains face computational constraints that force approximation

frameworks.name: inductive bias vs approximation bias
frameworks.one_line: Distinguishes belief constraints from process-of-thought constraints

core_question: How can rational design principles explain systematic errors that arise from approximating Bayesian inference under computational constraints?
key_assumptions: Brains face limited data and stringent computational constraints
Inductive biases constrain beliefs about the world
Approximation biases constrain the process of thought itself
key_findings: Bayes' rule provides a solution to limited-data inference by combining priors with observations
Approximation of intractable Bayesian computation yields systematic errors
interpretations: Errors from approximation biases can be understood as the cost of rational design under resource limits
Inductive biases and approximation biases are distinct sources of constraint on cognition
